---
title: "Global Sustainable Energy Trends (2000–2020)"
subtitle: "commit or quit"
author:
  - Shakshi Shah
  - Annamalai Vikash Raam Manoj
  - Siyuan Yin
  - Xuanning Zhang
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: moon
    slide-number: true
    transition: fade
    incremental: false
    embed-resources: true
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

## Problem Introduction {.smaller}

- Topic: renewable energy adoption and CO2 emissions
- Period: 2000–2020
- Countries: China, India, United States, Australia

::: fragment

**Research question:**  
How did renewable energy adoption and carbon emissions change across these major economies?

The project investigates how different approaches to energy transition have shaped carbon emission patterns over the past two decades. Renewable energy adoption increased across all four countries, but higher renewable energy use did not always result in lower carbon emissions.

:::

---

## Dataset Description {.smaller}

* *Source:* Global Sustainable Energy Dataset
* *Coverage:* 176 countries (2000–2020)
* *Observations:* 3,649 country-year records
* *Key Variables:*

  * Renewable energy share (% of total energy consumption)
  * CO₂ emissions (kt)

Why these variables?

* Renewable energy share → energy transition
* CO₂ emissions → environmental impact
* Together, they reveal how energy transitions affect emissions.

---

## Methodology {.smaller}

- Dataset
  - Global Sustainable Energy Dataset

- Countries
  - Australia, China, India, United States

- Variables
  - Renewable energy share (%)
  - CO₂ emissions (kt)

- Analysis
  - Data cleaning in R
  - Trend analysis (2000–2020)
  - 2019 comparison visualisations

---

## Results: CO₂ Emissions Trend {.smaller}

![](../Figures/co2_trend.png){fig-align="center" width="75%"}

* China experienced the largest increase in CO₂ emissions
* India’s emissions increased steadily over the study period
* United States emissions showed an overall decline
* Australia maintained comparatively low and stable emission levels


---

## Results: Renewable Energy Trend {.smaller}

![](../Figures/renewable_trend.png){fig-align="center"}

- India had the highest renewable energy share
- China declined first, then recovered slightly after 2012
- Australia and the United States increased slowly

---

## Results: 2019 Comparison {.smaller}

![Facet plot of CO₂ emmission VS renewable energy share](../Figures/facet_2019.png){fig-align="center"}

- India had the highest renewable energy share
- China had the highest CO2 emissions
- Renewable energy share alone did not explain emissions

---

## Discussion {.smaller}

- Renewable energy and CO2 emissions did not always move together
- Higher renewable share did not automatically mean lower emissions
- Emissions were also affected by:
  - population size
  - industrial activity
  - economic scale
  - energy demand
  - fossil fuel dependence


---

## Recommendations {.smaller}

- Continue investing in renewable energy infrastructure
- Improve grid infrastructure and energy storage
- Combine renewable energy policies with low-carbon policies
- Focus on high-emission sectors:
  - power generation
  - transport
  - heavy industry

---

## Conclusion {.smaller}

- Renewable energy adoption and carbon emissions do not always move in the same direction
- Carbon emissions are influenced by multiple factors beyond renewable energy use
- Each country has a different energy and economic context
- A complete analysis should consider renewable energy, fossil fuel use, energy demand, and national differences
